I can't remember which year it was. I think that might have been 83, 84, 82, 83, around that time. They said they spent the whole year there. It was great. We talked many times. And then Don came to all of this in a different way, Don Simons.

that the people watching this might might or might not know about the famous vet that was made between Don Simons and Don Brown. Don Brown who later wrote Human Universals Yeah, but which was the book that got me. Yeah, that was the book that got me. first book that got you. So Don Simons told Brown that he said I'm going to write a book about the evolution of human sexuality and he said I think there's going to be some universals of human sexuality.
And I'm going to go through all the ethnographic records. I'm going to look at this from an evolutionary point of view, etc. And Don Brown, who was trained as a political anthropologist, Don Brown says, any universal you think you find, I'm going to be able to find an exception. And Simon says, I don't think so. And Don Brown says, I'm sure I will be able to. So they made a bet.
about that and they bet a pitcher of beer um and um after simon's wrote what he wrote and did his research brown couldn't find any exceptions to the things that simon said and so acknowledged that simon's clearly had won the bet and then brown started asking himself why was i so sure
Why was I so sure? Because there had been a tradition in anthropology of looking both at universals and Hmm. at things that varied from culture to culture. But the field had sort of changed more into being interested only in variation. And so when he started, why was I so sure that there would be no universals? And he.
In the course that I started reading more of the history of anthropology and that's why he wrote Human Universals, which he dedicated to Simons. So that's where the book that got you into this field came from. It was his thinking about the bet and Yeah. the fact that he made the bet in the first place. And actually they hadn't done the official.
a picture of beer thing yet. And so in our first or second year here at UCSB, there was an anthro picnic party at, in the hills where Don Brown and Don Simons lived. And there was a ceremonial giving of the picture of beer from Brown to Simons. So I got to, I got to see that. That's fantastic.
It was, it historical moment was, but yeah yeah, that's where it came from. That's such a lovely story. I feel like

of how to respond in in Chile um I had been too influenced by Tversky and Kahneman I mean even though I thought that they were wrong about lots of things and you know I and John and Gerd Gigerenzer had done research showing that when you put the same probability problems when you express them in frequencies and absolute frequencies natural frequencies that people do very well so I knew that Tversky and Kahneman were
They're not wrong about the phenomena. If you don't do that, people will be bad at conditional probabilities. being transitive like having transit preferences which people always explain as if you like A better than B and B better than C then you'll like A better than C although that's a terrible way of putting it because A, B and C are not things like John's taller than Nike and Nike's Yeah, taller than Lita so they're bundles of goods yes, like you know yeah.
Five avocados and three tomatoes and 20 tomatoes and five avocados. It's bundles A, B, and C are bundle of goods. So it's very not communicated well to people who are not in economics. did research on that yeah Oh, interesting. footbridge in the trolley problem and that they're fighting and that's how well we and so kudos and that that's that's that's not going to be true people are going to be able to make trade -offs between different kinds of moral values and but you need to have a different kind of
a moral dilemma in order to even test that because nobody even gives you the opportunity to because you only have either push the guy off the bridge in front of the trolley to save the five people or don't push the guy and let the five people die it's only two options there and anyway he said I'm sure it's going to work out that people will not only be able to make trade -offs but they'll make rational trade-offs and I said but there's all this stuff in psychology he says no no that's what's going to happen
And he was completely right. Hmm He was gloriously right, to my surprise. So, you know, people behave exactly that they're doing what economists talk about when they talk about generating a utility function and then using whatever options are available to you, the costs and benefits of the options available to you to choose which is the best option.
The economists are not psychological about it, though. The economists, they say that they are agnostic about the mind. So when they give...
theories like this about utility maximization it's not about what's going on in an individual's mind which is very interesting and it took me years to figure out what the hell they were talking about I would go to these these behavioral economics workshops here that would happen once every two years that Gary Tarnas would would put on who also died recently everybody's dying all around me it's really
frustrating and upsetting but I would go to these and when I could and people would there would be these 40 -minute talks the first 20 minutes would be a beautiful experiment like some sort of behavioral economics experiment it might be in a lab it might be in the field with gorgeous results and anybody I know in psychology would have been thrilled to have done these experiments themselves
very interpretable results and then the second 20 minutes they'd be writing some full utility function on a board and and like I'd be zoning out during I don't think and so I asked people afterwards I said
what is this utility function what is it said is this is this a theory of the computation is this a like a david marr kind of i explain that is this a a theory of what problem mind is trying to solve and the economist and this was the economist it's not somebody you know day but there's a economist who is friendly to psychology and so he said no
It has nothing to do with the human mind. And I said, but you do think that individuals are making the decisions in these experiments. I said, yes, of course. And I said, you do think it has something to do with their brains? And he said, yes, of course. And he said, but the utility function has nothing to do with that. Yeah. I was thinking, what is it?
And actually, it turns out he's right, and that is the traditional view, and it has nothing to do with what's going on in the mind, in Yeah. the way economists think about it. The way economists think about it, their problem is different. Their problem is usually understanding a market.
